Author: Alin Muntean
Title: Unboxing Schrödinger’s Dataset: The real level of IP risk in AI generated code
Text:
AI is revolutionizing software development, but for engineering leaders in highly regulated industries, the legal risks associated with AI-generated code remain a major roadblock. The uncertainty around intellectual property (IP) liability—especially as AI code generation models are trained on vast and undisclosed datasets—creates a complex challenge for CIOs and technology executives looking to integrate AI into their development processes.
A recent 2025 RBC CIO survey found that nearly 90% of enterprise engineering teams plan to adopt AI for software development by the end of the year. Yet, 38% of respondents identified copyright infringement as their number one concern. This apprehension isn’t unfounded—current legal frameworks are still evolving, and lawsuits against AI tools like GitHub Copilot are shaping enterprise risk policies.
So, how real is the risk? Is adopting AI code assistants an unavoidable legal gamble, or is there a way to gain productivity without sacrificing compliance?
This article takes a deep dive into the true level of IP liability associated with AI-generated code, leveraging recent research from Carnegie Mellon University to separate fact from fiction. More importantly, it outlines a clear and practical framework for AI adoption—one that ensures compliance without forcing teams to trade off performance or privacy.
At Tabnine, we’ve built the industry’s most secure AI software development platform, trusted by enterprises with the strictest legal and security requirements. Through inference-time protection via Provenance and Attribution and training-time protection in our purpose-built Tabnine Protected model, we offer an AI adoption path that eliminates the risk of noncompliant code injections while maximizing engineering efficiency.
If you’re tasked with leading an AI initiative but are navigating concerns about legal liability, this article will equip you with the insights and strategies you need to move forward with confidence. It’s time to unbox Schrödinger’s dataset—and ensure your AI journey is both productive and protected.
For organizations evaluating AI for software development, the inherent IP liability present in LLM training data is a little bit like Schrödinger’s cat: it both is and isn’t alive In other words, it’s not always clear what data LLMs are trained on, and teams already reaping the rewards of AI may be reluctant to look inside the box. But the time has come to open the box and examine the reality—and when you do, you’ll find the cat is… mostly free of IP liabilities.
A study from Carnegie Mellon University, released in August 2024 and titled “A First Look at License Compliance Capability of LLMs in Code Generation”, offers critical insights. Researchers found that while LLMs can generate license-protected code, leading to potential IP violations, the actual occurrence of this is limited. Across all models studied, the frequency of generating code snippets that had a striking similarity score on the LiCoEval benchmark to license-protected code was between 0.88% and 2.01%—far lower than commonly feared.
Source: A first look at license compliance capabilities of LLMs in Code Generation
(The LicCoEval benchmark evaluation examined full functions and used the following factors to evaluate the string similarity of the code generated by the LLM against protected code: BLEU-4, Jaccard similarity based on minihash, similarity based on edit distance, number of function body lines, cyclomatic complexity, and comment similarity.)
Understanding the risk is only half the equation—taking proactive steps to mitigate it is what matters.
Tabnine has worked with many organizations concerned about IP liability and has helped engineering teams navigate highly restrictive AI policies, even providing teams with legal policies banning LLMs outright with a compliant pathway to AI adoption. Our tailored AI software development platform gives you full control over defining your specific performance, protection, and privacy needs when using LLMs for software development.
Our AI software development platform goes beyond the capabilities of generic AI code assistants effectively de-risking your use of AI through two distinct protection mechanisms: inference-time protection and training-time protection delivering the industry’s most comprehensive protection from IP liability in our category.
Inference-time protection safeguards you from IP liability during generation. At Tabnine, we call this capability Provenance and Attribution. When enabled, Tabnine checks AI-generated code against publicly visible code, flagging matches and referencing the source and its license for you to decide if the code meets your specific policies. You also have control over prioritizing the sources that Tabnine checks against. This feature works with any third-party LLM.
But Provenance and Attribution offers more than that. Tabnine customers can set their organizational standards for license compliance, eliminating the need for individual users to make those decisions. Admins can set license restrictions that prevent any third-party LLM you use from injecting code that matches noncompliant licenses into your software, codifying your standards.
Using Provenance and Attribution along with your preferred and approved performant third-party model creates a pathway for you to safely use AI without sacrificing protection for performance. With Tabnine, you also don’t need to trade off privacy. Tabnine Enterprise can be deployed on-premises, in a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), or via secure SaaS. Regardless of which deployment modality you choose, the use of third-party LLMs is completely configurable to meet your organization’s specific requirements.
If inference time protection is not a viable path forward for AI adoption, we can meet your needs with training time protection by giving you access to a model that was never exposed to non-compliant code and is completely free of risk. Tabnine Protected is a comparatively smaller model trained exclusively on permissively licensed code, purpose built for software development, and designed to offer teams with the most restrictive standards a pathway to AI adoption. The entire training data set used for Tabnine Protected can be obtained on request through our trust center.
However you choose to deploy AI, using inference-time protection gives you the benefit of leveraging the most performant LLM that can be approved by your organization’s legal and security team to power Tabnine. The most secure and private approach is to deploy a performant open-source model on-premises in conjunction with Tabnine. An alternative is to deploy in a VPC and connect to a private endpoint.
Regardless of which deployment option or third-party LLM you opt for, you benefit from the protection of Provenance and Attribution and organizational governance over what licenses are deemed acceptable—providing you with comprehensive protection against IP liability risk.
Tabnine provides an AI software development platform tailored to enterprise needs, ensuring you never have to choose between performance, privacy, and protection. If you’re ready to explore AI adoption with full compliance and peace of mind, we’re here to help you take the next step with confidence.
